caveats: a: The occupation proxy aggregates several machinery groups and is not six-digit.
a: Task exposure is inferred rather than observed.
a: The residual code ranges from printing presses and additive systems to textile, plastics, chemical, and kiln equipment.
rho: No five-year implementation measure for 333248 was found.
rho: Data maturity and repeatability vary too widely for a single adoption rate to describe every subsegment well.
e: The provided LMM firm count is missing and must not be replaced, so an eligible-firm total cannot be derived.
e: The residual NAICS category is unusually heterogeneous and public records may not reveal whether revenue is recurring.
s5: No six-digit transaction-rate or ownership-age series was located.
s5: Because n is a declared dataset gap, even a credible probability cannot produce a transferable-firm count.
s5: Public deal data overrepresent larger strategic machinery platforms.
q: Contract structure and revenue mix were not observed.
q: Commercial retention varies widely across commodity equipment, proprietary systems, aftermarket parts, and engineering-to-order projects.
d5: BLS output is a grouped machinery proxy, not 333248 demand.
d5: Capital-equipment orders are more volatile than underlying industrial output.
d5: The category's product mix changed between 2017 and 2022 NAICS, complicating historical comparisons.
o: No direct operator-required measure exists.
o: The physical-operator conclusion is strong, but the responsible operator may shift from a domestic specialist to an importer or integrator.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Quoted and purchase-order sales of engineered capital equipment across chemical, plastics, rubber, textile, printing, additive, surface-treatment, kiln, and numerous other processes, with recurring revenue from repeat models, spares, wear parts, rebuilds, retrofits, controls, and support.
lens.deviation_from_default: The residual code spans many unrelated machinery end markets and includes episodic custom projects. The lens retains only manufacturers with repeat model, customer, parts, rebuild, upgrade, or support revenue so the recurring outsourced-supply population is coherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Agricultural, construction, mining, food, semiconductor, sawmill, woodworking, and paper machinery assigned to other codes; captive equipment departments; pure distributors; shell entities; financing vehicles; and one-off project shops with no repeat product or installed-base relationship.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers of otherwise-unclassified industrial machinery that repeatedly supply external factories through standard or engineered equipment, replacement parts, tooling, controls upgrades, rebuilds, and technical support.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation shares for the combined 3331, 3332, 3334, and 3339 machinery group, not 333248 or task exposure. I map portions of engineering, drafting, configuration, estimating, quoting, controls programming, procurement, planning, quality, documentation, customer support, machining setup, and diagnostics to AI capability and wage-weight higher-paid technical work. The combined group may dilute the code's custom engineering intensity; conversely, 48.45% production employment and heavy assembly, metalworking, machining, and welding mean whole occupations cannot be treated as substitutable.
caveats: The occupation proxy aggregates several machinery groups and is not six-digit.
Task exposure is inferred rather than observed.
The residual code ranges from printing presses and additive systems to textile, plastics, chemical, and kiln equipment.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study across representative 333248 subsegments, separating engineering and controls, quoting, procurement, machining, welding, assembly, inspection, installation, field service, parts, and administration, and documenting current CAD/PLM/ERP/CNC automation.
a.rationale: The BLS proxy is 48.45% production employment, including 19.37% assemblers/fabricators and 16.61% metal/plastic workers, with significant welding and machining. A smaller but higher-paid layer of engineering, management, business, sales, planning, inventory, and customer support offers document- and data-rich tasks. AI can improve design reuse, specifications, quotes, programming, inspection, scheduling, manuals, diagnostics, and parts forecasting; physical fabrication, assembly, installation, and field work remain dominant.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for the combined machinery group containing 333248 to grow 1.0% annually from 2024 to 2034 and all machinery manufacturing at 1.1%. Applying roughly that rate for five years implies about 1.05. The residual code's end markets range from growing additive, plastics, chemical, and automation applications to mature textile and printing equipment, so the broad proxy may overstate declining niches and understate growth niches; the range is widened substantially.
caveats: BLS output is a grouped machinery proxy, not 333248 demand.
Capital-equipment orders are more volatile than underlying industrial output.
The category's product mix changed between 2017 and 2022 NAICS, complicating historical comparisons.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year shipment, order, and backlog data for each material 333248 subsegment from Census product tables, machinery trade groups, public OEM filings, and customer capex plans, weighted by current industry revenue.
d5.rationale: Factories continue to replace aging equipment, automate labor-constrained processes, add controls and traceability, and buy parts and retrofits. Demand is diversified but exposed to capital-spending cycles, interest rates, reshoring uncertainty, imports, customer consolidation, and structural decline in some printing or textile applications. The most defensible central case is modest growth with high dispersion.

caveats: The provided LMM firm count is missing and must not be replaced, so an eligible-firm total cannot be derived.
The residual NAICS category is unusually heterogeneous and public records may not reveal whether revenue is recurring.
e.change_evidence: A complete firm-level universe with normalized EBITDA, primary machinery activity, captive status, repeat-customer and aftermarket revenue, installed-base size, backlog quality, model standardization, transferable IP, and customer concentration.
e.rationale: Firms with repeated equipment models or meaningful installed-base parts, rebuild, retrofit, controls, and support revenue qualify. Exclusions include isolated engineering-to-order projects, captive departments, pure distributors, nontransferable businesses dependent on one inventor or customer, misclassified firms, and candidates outside the normalized EBITDA band.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) at ancestor 33324: labor compensation (L02) $12,802.3M / sectoral output (T30) $39,460.3M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $373,440 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 33324); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: No direct operator-required measure exists.
The physical-operator conclusion is strong, but the responsible operator may shift from a domestic specialist to an importer or integrator.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews across major subsegments on insourced engineering and maintenance, imports, remote software support, additive spares, OEM-independent controls, and hardware-free process improvements.
o.rationale: Year-five demand is for physical machinery, parts, retrofits, controls, and accountable engineering, fabrication, commissioning, and support. AI and software can improve or replace selected control and documentation functions but cannot supply most of the current hardware basket. Residual displacement comes from customer insourcing, imports, software-only upgrades, additive spare parts, and standardized modular equipment sold without ongoing OEM support.

caveats: Contract structure and revenue mix were not observed.
Commercial retention varies widely across commodity equipment, proprietary systems, aftermarket parts, and engineering-to-order projects.
q.change_evidence: Representative equipment, parts, retrofit, and service contracts showing fixed-price duration, escalation clauses, guarantees, warranty exposure, annual discounts, bid competition, aftermarket margins, and pricing after documented productivity gains.
q.rationale: Proprietary machinery, controls, application know-how, installed-base compatibility, urgent spares, and qualification can protect savings. New capital projects are heavily negotiated, often competitively bid, and may carry performance guarantees, warranties, and open component comparisons. Retention should be stronger in proprietary parts and retrofits than in new-system bids.

caveats: No five-year implementation measure for 333248 was found.
Data maturity and repeatability vary too widely for a single adoption rate to describe every subsegment well.
rho.change_evidence: Subsegment-specific evidence on PLM/ERP and telemetry coverage, AI pilot conversion, engineering and quote hours saved, code and drawing error rates, inspection accuracy, service outcomes, integration costs, and safety-validation results.
rho.rationale: Design copilots, specification retrieval, quote configuration, computer vision, production planning, service diagnostics, and parts forecasting can be operational within five years. Implementation is constrained by highly varied subsegments, low-volume custom machinery, legacy drawings and controls, sparse failure data, machine safety, customer validation, export controls, cybersecurity, small engineering teams, and required human sign-off.

caveats: No six-digit transaction-rate or ownership-age series was located.
Because n is a declared dataset gap, even a credible probability cannot produce a transferable-firm count.
Public deal data overrepresent larger strategic machinery platforms.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, CapIQ, GF Data, Axial, and industrial-equipment broker control transactions from 2016-2025, classified to the many 333248 subsegments and matched to an eligible-firm denominator, plus owner-age and succession interviews.
s5.rationale: Specialist OEM succession and strategic interest in proprietary equipment and installed bases support a low-twenties five-year transfer probability. Family/internal succession, indefinite holds, minority recapitalizations, failed sale processes, closures, and asset sales without a transferable operating firm are excluded.

principal_driver: Proprietary installed bases and complex engineering workflows support repeat aftermarket revenue and data-rich AI use cases.
principal_weakness: The missing firm denominator and extreme subsegment heterogeneity prevent a defensible estimate of opportunity scale.

sources.supports: BLS reports production occupations at 48.45% of the combined machinery workforce, assemblers/fabricators at 19.37%, metal/plastic workers at 16.61%, and detailed welding, machining, inspection, engineering, office, and support roles.
sources.title: May 2023 Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Machinery Manufacturing (3331, 3332, 3334, and 3339)

sources.supports: DOE describes manufacturing AI applications including generative design, automated visual quality control, advanced characterization, operations optimization, and predictive maintenance using IoT sensors.
sources.title: AI for Energy: Opportunities for a Modern Grid and Clean Energy Economy

sources.supports: BLS projects annual real-output growth of 1.0% for the grouped machinery category covering 3331, 3332, 3334, and 3339 and 1.1% for all machinery manufacturing.
sources.title: Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034

sources.supports: Census defines the residual industry and lists diverse products including additive, chemical-processing, plastics and rubber, textile, printing and bookbinding, kiln, electroplating, and other industrial machinery.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Search: 333248 All Other Industrial Machinery Manufacturing

title: All Other Industrial Machinery Manufacturing
